nuggets.headline: Daft Punk's 'Homework' was recorded with a surprisingly lo-fi and unconventional multi-step process that involved recording to tape, sampling themselves, and then re-sequencing.
nuggets.text: For their debut album, Daft Punk first recorded their hardware instruments through a Mackie mixer and an Alesis 3630 compressor onto a Panasonic SV-3700 DAT machine. They would then sample these recordings into a Roland S-760 sampler, where they would chop up the audio. The final tracks were sequenced from the S-760 using a Mac and recorded back to the DAT machine.

source.title: Here's a list of all the gear Daft Punk used to make 'Homework'
source.quoteSnippet: “The second step is directly putting the sounds from the DAT into the S-760 sampler and editing/cutting the two tracks into however many pieces, like hard disk recording. “Lastly, we recorded it directly from the S-760 to the DAT. From here we do the rest with the Macintosh.”

nuggets.headline: The track's iconic robotic vocal wasn't a vocoder, but a talk box, and the filtered, underwater-like intro was likely created using the built-in effects of their sampler.
nuggets.text: The phrase "around the world" is processed through a talk box, an effects pedal that allows a musician to shape the sound of an instrument with their mouth. This technique was heavily influenced by the '80s funk artist Roger Troutman of Zapp. The muffled, low-pass filter effect heard in the song's introduction is thought to have been created using the onboard filter of the Roland S-760 sampler during the recording process.

source.title: How Daft Punk made "Homework": A layman-friendly walkthrough of the tools and techniques that made Daft Punk's first album
source.quoteSnippet: At times, they also used the S-760's built in filter, as it was recording to tape. You can hear this on Revolution 909 and the intro to Around the World for sure.

nuggets.headline: If the raw, jacking pulse of 'Homework' speaks to you, then you need to dig into the work of Chicago house pioneer Paul Johnson.
nuggets.text: Paul Johnson is the very first artist Daft Punk name-check in their tribute track 'Teachers'. His raw, rhythm-driven tracks released on labels like Dance Mania, featuring pummeling kicks and chopped-up samples, were a clear blueprint for Daft Punk's early sound. Listening to Johnson's 1996 track 'Hear the Music' feels like hearing a direct ancestor of the repetitive, hypnotic grooves found all over 'Homework'.

source.title: Paul Johnson (producer) - Wikipedia
source.quoteSnippet: Paul Johnson is the first name mentioned in the Daft Punk song "Teachers", a tribute to several of Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo and Thomas Bangalter's house music influences.
